summary: Cavalier poet known for 'To Althea, from Prison' and 'To Lucasta, Going to the Wars.' A member of the 'Tribe of Ben.' Associated with the English Civil War and royalist cause.

works.name: To Althea, from Prison
works.description: Written while imprisoned. Famous for 'Stone walls do not a prison make, / Nor iron bars a cage.' The speaker sings of 'the sweetness, mercy, majesty / And glories of my King.' Declares that 'fishes that tipple in the deep' and 'birds that wanton in the air' 'know no such liberty' as he does. 'Committed linnets' sing with 'shriller throat.' Flowing cups 'with no allaying Thames.' Ends 'Angels alone that soar above / Enjoy such liberty.'

clues.clue: 'Stone walls do not a prison make, / Nor iron bars a cage'
examples: 'Stone walls do not a prison make'

clues.clue: 'Fishes that tipple in the deep' / 'birds that wanton in the air' know no such liberty
examples: 'fishes that tipple in the Deep... know no such liberty'

clues.clue: Speaker sings 'the sweetness, mercy, majesty / And glories of my King'
examples: 'the sweetness, Mercy, Majesty / And glories of my King'

clues.clue: 'Angels alone that soar above / Enjoy such liberty'
examples: 'Angels alone that soar above / Enjoy such liberty'

works.name: To Lucasta, Going to the Wars
works.description: Speaker apologizes for leaving his lover to fight for Charles I in the English Civil War. Famous ending: 'I could not love thee, Dear, so much, / Lov'd I not Honour more.' Speaker declares 'a new mistress now I chase' and 'with a stronger faith embrace / A sword, a horse, a shield.' Lucasta is probably based on Lucy Sacheverell, nicknamed 'Lux Casta' ('pure light'). Inspired by Lovelace's service under George Goring in the Bishops' Wars.

clues.clue: 'I could not love thee, Dear, so much, / Lov'd I not Honour more'
examples: 'I could not love thee, Dear, so much, / Lov'd I not Honour more'

clues.clue: Speaker leaves for battle — embraces 'a sword, a horse, a shield'
examples: 'with a stronger faith embrace / A sword, a horse, a shield'

clues.clue: Lucasta based on Lucy Sacheverell ('pure light')

works.name: Other Poems
works.description: 'The Grasshopper' is dedicated to his 'Noble Friend' Charles Cotton, depicting an insect getting drunk on an 'oaten beard.' 'To Amarantha, That She Would Dishevel Her Hair' is a carpe diem poem. 'The Snail' calls the title animal 'wise emblem of our politic world.' 'The Scrutinie' notes 'I loved the addressee for twelve hours but now must be sated with Varietie.' 'To Lucasta: Her Reserved Looks' describes Lucasta's face as 'an angel here, the devil there.' Andrew Marvell commended Lovelace's poems.

examples: 'The Grasshopper,' a poem dedicated to his 'Noble Friend' Charles Cotton

clues.clue: 'To Amarantha, That She Would Dishevel Her Hair' — carpe diem poem
examples: beseeching Amarantha to perform this action

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Cavalier poet who fought for Charles I. Member of the 'Tribe of Ben' (followers of Ben Jonson). Imprisoned during the English Civil War, inspiring 'To Althea, from Prison.' Served under George Goring in the Bishops' Wars. Cavalier poetry associated with carpe diem themes.

clues.clue: Cavalier poet — member of 'Tribe of Ben'
Fought for Charles I; imprisoned during the Civil War
examples: fought under George Goring in the Bishops' Wars

comprehensive_summary:
Richard Lovelace (1617-1657) was an English Cavalier poet, one of the 'Sons of Ben' (followers of Ben Jonson), known primarily for two poems written during the English Civil War.

'To Althea, from Prison' was written while Lovelace was imprisoned for his royalist sympathies. Its most famous lines are 'Stone walls do not a prison make, / Nor iron bars a cage.' The speaker sings of 'the sweetness, mercy, majesty / And glories of my King,' and declares that 'fishes that tipple in the deep' and 'birds that wanton in the air' 'know no such liberty' as he does. Only 'Angels alone that soar above / Enjoy such liberty.'

'To Lucasta, Going to the Wars' is addressed to his lover as the speaker departs for battle, concluding 'I could not love thee, Dear, so much, / Lov'd I not Honour more.' The speaker embraces 'a sword, a horse, a shield.' Lucasta is probably based on Lucy Sacheverell, whose nickname means 'pure light.' The poem was inspired by Lovelace's service under George Goring in the Bishops' Wars.

Other poems include 'The Grasshopper' (dedicated to Charles Cotton), 'To Amarantha, That She Would Dishevel Her Hair' (a carpe diem poem), and 'The Snail.' Andrew Marvell wrote a commendatory poem for Lovelace.
